decisions.deployment_strategy: Phased: Foundation → Data → Edge → Platform (4 phases, ~80 min deploy)
decisions.redis_tier: Premium P4 (53 GB) — optimized from Enterprise 128 GB

open_findings.description: Redis 128 GB tier requirement — resolved: Premium P4 (53 GB) for MVP, saves $1,845/mo vs Enterprise. Upgrade path documented.
AKS vs Azure Container Apps — resolved: AKS selected. RFP specifies Managed Kubernetes + 8 vCPU nodes; 15 microservices need full K8s control.
No explicit budget — resolved: Bottom-up volumetric estimate of ~$9,280/mo (all envs) within $12K envelope at 77% utilization.
Payment integration model not specified — resolved: Tokenized gateway assumed (SAQ-A scope). Card data never stored on Azure.

Foundation deployment blocked: modules/keyvault.bicep sets networkAcls.bypass to 'None' while the AVM deployment requires AzureServices bypass when enabledForDeployment is true. Partial resources were created in rg-contoso-service-hub-run-3-dev.

Platform deployment remains blocked after foundation, data, and edge succeeded. AKS preflight still fails in rg-contoso-service-hub-run-3-dev because the managed cluster service CIDR overlaps the existing VNet address space and the AVM path continues to send an invalid linuxConfiguration.ssh.publicKeys.keyData payload.

decision_log.title: Complexity classification
decision_log.rationale: 15 Azure services (>8 threshold), 3 environments (Dev/Staging/Prod), GDPR mandatory + potential PCI-DSS, 99.9% SLA, 40x transaction growth
alternatives: standard

decision_log.title: Budget estimation from RFP volumetrics
decision_log.rationale: RFP does not specify budget. Estimated from 15 proposed cloud services, indicative volumetrics (Table 2), and enterprise-tier requirements across 3 environments (Prod ~EUR 7-8K, Staging ~EUR 3-4K, Dev ~EUR 1-1.5K)
alternatives: Request explicit budget from Contoso before proceeding

decision_log.title: Redis 128 GB tier selection
decision_log.choice: Azure Cache for Redis Enterprise tier (128 GB)
decision_log.rationale: RFP Table 2 specifies 128 GB in-memory cache. Azure Premium tier maxes at 120 GB (P5), so Enterprise tier required. Flagged as open question due to significant cost impact.
alternatives: Premium P4 (53 GB)
Premium P5 (120 GB)
Enterprise E100 (100 GB)

decision_log.title: Container platform selection
decision_log.rationale: RFP specifies Standard 8 vCPU VMs for container engine, implying VM-backed node pools (AKS). Section 4.1 also lists Managed Kubernetes. Flagged for architect confirmation.
alternatives: Azure Container Apps

decision_log.title: Payment integration model
decision_log.choice: Tokenized payment via external gateway (SAQ-A scope)
decision_log.rationale: RFP mentions payments but does not detail integration model. Tokenized gateway keeps card data off Azure, reducing PCI-DSS scope to SAQ-A.
alternatives: Direct card processing (full PCI-DSS scope)

decision_log.title: Budget estimation methodology — bottom-up volumetric
decision_log.rationale: No explicit RFP budget. Bottom-up estimate from individual SKU pricing × volumetrics × environment scaling factors (Prod 100%, Staging 60%, Dev 25%). Azure public retail pricing for swedencentral, March 2026. Conservative: pay-as-you-go, no RI/SP, no egress optimization. ±15% variance expected.
alternatives: Top-down estimate from requirements doc (~$12K/mo)
Request explicit budget from Contoso procurement

decision_log.title: Redis tier selection — Premium P4 (53 GB) for MVP
decision_log.choice: Azure Cache for Redis Premium P4 (53 GB) at $1,455/mo instead of Enterprise E100 (128 GB) at $3,300/mo
decision_log.rationale: 128 GB is over-provisioned for 5K initial users (~15 GB estimated session state + cache utilization). Premium P4 provides 3.5× headroom. Saves $1,845/mo ($22,140/yr). Clear upgrade path: monitor cache utilization, trigger Enterprise upgrade when sustained >70% (projected Month 8-10 at ~30K concurrent sessions). P4 supports zone-redundancy and AOF persistence for RPO compliance.
alternatives: Enterprise E100 (100 GB, $3,300/mo)
Premium P5 (120 GB, $2,912/mo)
Premium P3 (26 GB, $728/mo — too small for growth)

decision_log.title: AKS selected over Azure Container Apps
decision_log.choice: AKS Standard with 3× Standard_D8s_v5 nodes across 3 AZs
decision_log.rationale: RFP explicitly specifies 'Managed Kubernetes' and 'Standard 8 vCPU nodes' — indicating VM-backed node pools (AKS). 15 microservices require full Kubernetes control: custom networking (CNI), pod-level autoscaling (HPA), Cluster Autoscaler (3→6 nodes), namespace-level RBAC, and ingress controller customization. Container Apps would limit networking control and pod scheduling flexibility needed for this enterprise workload.
alternatives: Azure Container Apps (simpler but insufficient K8s control for 15 microservices)

decision_log.title: Challenger fix: Azure AD B2C replaced with Microsoft Entra External ID
decision_log.choice: Microsoft Entra External ID for all CIAM references
decision_log.rationale: Azure AD B2C was deprecated for new customers as of May 1, 2025. For a 2026 greenfield project, Microsoft Entra External ID is the successor CIAM platform. All references to B2C (identity, cost, maturity, implementation) updated throughout 02-architecture-assessment.md and 03-des-cost-estimate.md. Free tier (50K MAU) pricing unchanged.
alternatives: Azure AD B2C (deprecated for new tenants)

decision_log.title: Challenger fix: Blob GRS replaced with ZRS for GDPR compliance
decision_log.choice: Blob ZRS (zone-redundant storage) instead of GRS
decision_log.rationale: GRS replicates data to a paired region, violating the EU-only / no cross-region replication GDPR stance defined in requirements. ZRS keeps all 3 copies within the same region (swedencentral) across availability zones, maintaining zone-level redundancy without cross-region data movement. No cost impact — ZRS and GRS are similarly priced for Standard Hot tier.
alternatives: GRS (cross-region, violates GDPR no-cross-region rule)
LRS (single-zone, lower resilience)

decision_log.title: Challenger fix: APIM must be non-public behind Front Door
decision_log.choice: APIM deployed in internal VNet mode behind Front Door — no direct public access
decision_log.rationale: Architecture claims Front Door is the only public ingress, but APIM Standard tier exposes a public gateway endpoint by default. To enforce the zero-trust stance, APIM must use VNet integration in internal mode (or private endpoint) so Front Door origins connect via private IP. Added as security gap, recommendation, and network topology clarification.
alternatives: APIM with public endpoint + IP allowlisting (weaker security posture)

decision_log.title: Governance discovery completed from live Azure Policy assignments
decision_log.choice: Use live ARM REST discovery for tenant-enforced controls and carry uncovered security requirements as project baseline constraints
decision_log.rationale: Azure credentials were available in the automated E2E run. The active subscription exposed 21 policy assignments with relevant deny, audit, and modify coverage for tags, AKS, storage, PostgreSQL, Redis, and Key Vault. Some required controls such as EU-only locations, APIM internal mode, and AKS network policy were not directly enforced by tenant deny policies, so they were preserved as explicit plan adaptations.
alternatives: Fallback template-only governance artifact if Azure credentials were unavailable

decision_log.title: Implementation plan — phased deployment with 100% AVM coverage
decision_log.choice: 4-phase deployment (Foundation → Data → Edge → Platform) with 16 resource types across 14 AVM-backed modules, 3 environments, all 22 governance controls addressed
decision_log.rationale: All 16 resource types verified against AVM registry (100% coverage). Phased deployment ordered by dependency graph: foundation services (monitoring, networking, key vault, budget) first, data layer second (PostgreSQL, Redis, Storage with private endpoints), edge third (APIM internal + Front Door WAF), platform last (ACR, AKS, VM). Governance blockers (CTRL-001 RG tags, CTRL-002 AKS pool limit) resolved in plan; 10 warnings addressed in security configuration matrix.
alternatives: Single deployment (rejected: >15 resources, production/compliance workload)
5-phase with separate security phase (rejected: Key Vault deploys with foundation for early secret availability)

decision_log.title: Foundation deploy remediation applied in Bicep
decision_log.choice: Set Key Vault network ACL bypass to AzureServices and add an explicit dependency from VNet diagnostics to the VNet module
decision_log.rationale: The failed deployment surfaced two independent issues: Key Vault validation rejected networkAcls.bypass='None' when enabledForDeployment was true, and the initial networking failure was consistent with diagnostic settings racing the VNet creation because the scoped existing resource did not imply a dependency. After applying both fixes, bicep build, bicep lint, and azd provision --preview all succeeded against the partially deployed resource group.
alternatives: Disable enabledForDeployment in the Key Vault module
Retry deployment without code changes and rely on eventual ARM ordering

decision_log.title: Foundation deploy completed after Key Vault private DNS zone fix
decision_log.choice: Create the Key Vault private DNS zone inside modules/keyvault.bicep, link it to the foundation VNet, and feed the AVM private endpoint the created zone resource ID
decision_log.rationale: The next azd provision attempt failed with InvalidPrivateDnsZoneIds on the privateDnsZoneGroups child resource because main.bicep was passing a resourceId for privatelink.vaultcore.azure.net without creating that zone. Aligning Key Vault to the same DNS-zone creation pattern already used by PostgreSQL, Redis, and Storage resolved the final blocker. bicep build, bicep lint, azd provision --preview --no-prompt, and azd provision --no-prompt all succeeded afterward.
alternatives: Pre-create the private DNS zone out-of-band and keep passing a static resource ID
Remove the private DNS zone group and rely on manual DNS wiring after deployment

decision_log.title: Foundation, data, and edge phases completed; platform blocked at AKS
decision_log.choice: Continue with the successfully deployed foundation, data, and edge layers while recording the AKS platform blocker for a follow-up code pass
decision_log.rationale: The deployment sequence converged for monitoring, networking, Key Vault, data services, API Management, Front Door, identity, and ACR after multiple template remediations. The remaining platform blocker is isolated to the AKS module: Azure rejects the current service CIDR because it overlaps the VNet subnet space, and the managed-cluster AVM path still fails ssh.publicKeys.keyData validation despite explicit overrides.
alternatives: Keep retrying the same AKS deployment without changing the service CIDR design
Abort the entire deployment and roll back the already healthy foundation, data, and edge phases

skip_reasons: E2E automated run — plan review skipped per user directive
